feedback.text: Hi, Id like to suggest that the Digital Fairness Act include protections for players who lose access to games theyve bought simply because the publisher shuts down the servers. More and more games today are online-only, even if theyre mainly single-player. When companies decide a game isnt profitable anymore, they take the servers down, and the game just stops working. This has already happened with Harry Potter: Wizards Unite and the original Burnout Paradise, and there are many more examples. Whats most frustrating is that this practice takes away our right to actually own what we buy. Were paying full price for something that can disappear at any time. It feels less like ownership and more like renting access, only theres no clear end date or guarantee. Its part of a larger trend where companies are moving away from selling finished products, and instead chasing ongoing revenue. But that shouldnt come at the cost of fairness or basic consumer rights. I support what the Stop Killing Games initiative is asking for: that publishers should be required to have an end-of-life plan for online-only games, whether its an offline mode, community-hosted servers, or something else that lets people keep playing what they paid for. Thanks for listening, and for standing up for digital fairness. Best regards, Martin Zilak Slovakia
